They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Family tree == {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
=== Aso genealogy === {{Tree chart/start|align=center|summary=Aso family tree}} {{Tree chart| | | | | |EmJ|EmJ=[[Emperor Jimmu]]|}} {{Tree chart| | |,|-|-|-|+|-|-|-|.| }} {{Tree chart| |MOM||DAD| |DAISY|y| Lee|MOM=Kanakori no Kami |DAD={{ill|Kamuyaimimi|lt=Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto|simple}}|DAISY=Kunitatsu no Kami |Lee=Hime Miko no Kami|}} {{Tree chart| ||!| | | |!| | | | ,| -|^|- |.|}} {{Tree chart| |Koutou | | ME |y | SIS | |SHIN|y|YAYA| |Koutou=Imperial Family Line|ME=Takeiwatatsu no Mikoto|SIS=Asotsuhime no Mikoto|SHIN=Shinhiko no Mikoto|YAYA=Yahime no Kami |}} {{Tree chart| ||||| | | |!| | | ||| ,| -|^|- |.|}} {{Tree chart| || | | | | |MOM| | | | DAD|| DAISY |MOM=Hayamikatama no Mikoto|DAD=Wakahiko no kami|DAISY=Shinhime no Mikoto}} {{Tree chart| ||||| | | |!| | | ||| !| }} {{Tree chart| |||MOM| y| DAD|| | | DAISY |MOM=Wakahime no Kami|DAD=Hikomiko no Kami|DAISY=Aso Shrine Extended Family}} {{Tree chart| ||| | ||!|}} {{Tree chart| | | | | | MOM|MOM=''Current'' Aso Shrine Priests}} {{Tree chart/end}}<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Takano |first1=Tomoaki |title=History and Festivals of the Aso Shrine |last2=Uchimura |first2=Hiroaki |publisher=Aso Shrine |year=2006 |location=Aso Shrine, Ichinomiya, Aso City.}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Hikoyai|Hikoyai-mo-mikoto]] (彦八井耳命) - Some say that he was the brother, son, or the same person as {{Interlanguage link|Kamuyaimimi|lt=Kamuyaimimi|ja|神八井耳命}}. Some also believe that he is the national dragon god enshrined at [[Aso Shrine]].

== Further reading ==

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Prince (son of Emperor)" to "Imperial Prince"
revision.text:
{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}}
{{Infobox noble|reign-type=[[神武天皇]]の{{ill|Imperial Prince|ja|皇子|vi|Hoàng tử|zh|皇子|ko|황자|lt=Imperial Prince}}|birth_date=unknown|death_date=April 4th|death_place=[[Yamato Province]]|father=[[Jimmu]]|mother=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]}}
{{Nihongo|'''Kamuyaimimi'''|2=神八井耳命}}, is a figure in Japanese mythology and one of the three brothers born to [[Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] and his wife [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]].<ref name="Motoori2007">{{cite book |author=Norinaga Motoori |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Zk4EucRlvSwC&pg=PA191 |title=The Poetics of Motoori Norinaga: A Hermeneutical Journey |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-8248-3078-6 |page=191}}</ref><ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /><ref>{{Citation |title=Book III |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_III |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-03-09}} (called Kami-ya-wi-mimi in this source)</ref> He is best known for his role in the Assassination of [[Tagishimimi]], in which he and his brother Suizei killed Tagishimimi in order to protect Suizei from assassination.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=神八井耳命（カムヤイミミノミコト） |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2609.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}</ref>
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto was the elder brother of Suizei, and would have been expected to ascend the throne as emperor according to the lineage. However, he passed the throne on to his younger brother, Suizei, instead. This was seen as a humble and selfless act, similar to the "oath of subordination" between the brothers Yamayukihiko and Umiyukihiko.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also connected to [[Aso Shrine]], which is home to a deity named Takeban Ryujin (Taikei Watatsu). According to shrine lore, Takeban Ryujin is said to be a "child" of Kamuyaiminomikoto, and this is supported by the Kojiki, which states that "Aso-kun is a descendant of Kamuyaiminomikoto".<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> He was an ancestor of the [[Aso clan]]
In aqqition to his role in the Assassination of Tagishimimi, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also known for his courage and leadership. When the princes heard of Tagishimimi's plan to assassinate Suizei, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto immediately tried to kill Tagishimimi, but his limbs trembled and he was unable to do so. His younger brother, Kamununakahamiminomikoto, took his brother's weapon and went in to kill Tagishimimi instead.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
== Historical records == According to historical records, the Yamato Imperial Court dispatched influential members of the imperial family to the area of [[Shinano Province]] in order to control the powerful [[Suwa-no-Kami|Suwa no Kami]], who was descended from the Izumo and Koshi gods, and also because the area was a strategic point between the Sea of Japan and the East. One of these imperial family members was Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kuninomiyatsukos of Shinano and the son of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient record of Japanese history, as the one who firmly established the Emperor System.<ref name="archiveph-2023a">{{Cite web |title=archive.ph |url=https://archive.today/20230117162104/https://kodaishi.net/person/royalty/kamuyaimimi#selection-411.0-777.3 |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
The Kojiki also states that Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto appointed Takeiotatsu no Mikoto, a grandson of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, as the national governor. This appointment is believed to be the first time the king of Japan was appointed to the position of Kunizukuri. The palace of Shikinomizugaki was the palace of the 10th Emperor Sojin.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
The family of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is believed to have followed the four provincial generals, Ohiko-no-mikoto. The Shinano-no-kuninomiyatsukos are believed to have been buried in the Mori Shogunzuka Kofun, located on the opposite side of the Kawayanagi Shogunzuka Kofun.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
Hase Shrine in Shiozaki, Shinanoi, Nagano City, is dedicated to Yaimimi no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Shinano-no-kuninozukuri. After the age of Shintoism and Buddhism, the shrine became Hase Kannon. Along with Hase Temple in Yamato and Kamakura, Hase Temple is known as one of the three Hase Kannon temples in Japan. The temple was founded during the reign of Emperor Seimei (reigned 629-641), the father of Emperor Tenchi, and is said to be the oldest Hase temple in Japan.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Descendants == Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also the ancestor of a very large number of clans, as mentioned in the Kojiki. These include the Iwa-no-Kunino-no-Miyatsuko of Hitachinoku, the Naka-no-Kunino-Miyatsuko of Hitachi, the Funaki-no-Atahi of Ise, the Niwa-no-Omi of Owari, and the Shimada-no-Minamoto,<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> he is also said to be the ancestor of the [[Ō clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/743364863 |title=Nihon kodai shizoku jinmei jiten. |date=November 2010 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |others=Taro Sakamoto, Kunio Hirano, 太郎 坂本, 邦雄 平野 |isbn=978-4-642-01458-8 |chapter=神八井耳命（古代氏族） |trans-chapter=Kamuyaimimi (ancient clan) |oclc=743364863}}</ref> and the [[Aso clan]]. The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Family tree == {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
=== Aso genealogy === {{Tree chart/start|align=center|summary=Aso family tree}} {{Tree chart| | | | | |EmJ|EmJ=[[Emperor Jimmu]]|}} {{Tree chart| | |,|-|-|-|+|-|-|-|.| }} {{Tree chart| |MOM||DAD| |DAISY|y| Lee|MOM=Kanakori no Kami |DAD={{ill|Kamuyaimimi|lt=Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto|simple}}|DAISY=Kunitatsu no Kami |Lee=Hime Miko no Kami|}} {{Tree chart| ||!| | | |!| | | | ,| -|^|- |.|}} {{Tree chart| |Koutou | | ME |y | SIS | |SHIN|y|YAYA| |Koutou=Imperial Family Line|ME=Takeiwatatsu no Mikoto|SIS=Asotsuhime no Mikoto|SHIN=Shinhiko no Mikoto|YAYA=Yahime no Kami |}} {{Tree chart| ||||| | | |!| | | ||| ,| -|^|- |.|}} {{Tree chart| || | | | | |MOM| | | | DAD|| DAISY |MOM=Hayamikatama no Mikoto|DAD=Wakahiko no kami|DAISY=Shinhime no Mikoto}} {{Tree chart| ||||| | | |!| | | ||| !| }} {{Tree chart| |||MOM| y| DAD|| | | DAISY |MOM=Wakahime no Kami|DAD=Hikomiko no Kami|DAISY=Aso Shrine Extended Family}} {{Tree chart| ||| | ||!|}} {{Tree chart| | | | | | MOM|MOM=''Current'' Aso Shrine Priests}} {{Tree chart/end}}<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Takano |first1=Tomoaki |title=History and Festivals of the Aso Shrine |last2=Uchimura |first2=Hiroaki |publisher=Aso Shrine |year=2006 |location=Aso Shrine, Ichinomiya, Aso City.}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Hikoyai|Hikoyai-mo-mikoto]] (彦八井耳命) - Some say that he was the brother, son, or the same person as {{Interlanguage link|Kamuyaimimi|lt=Kamuyaimimi|ja|神八井耳命}}. Some also believe that he is the national dragon god enshrined at [[Aso Shrine]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further reading ==
* [https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_III] (called Kami-ya-wi-mimi in this source)
[[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:People of Jōmon-period Japan]] [[Category:Aso clan]] [[Category:Ō clan]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko position holders]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[en:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:皇子]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Kamuyaimimi]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] {{Aso Faith}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:qq]]

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}}
{{Infobox noble|reign-type=[[神武天皇]]の{{ill|Imperial Prince|ja|皇子|vi|Hoàng tử|zh|皇子|ko|황자|lt=Imperial Prince}}|birth_date=unknown|death_date=April 4th|death_place=[[Yamato Province]]|father=[[Jimmu]]|mother=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]}}
{{Nihongo|'''Kamuyaimimi'''|2=神八井耳命}}, is a figure in Japanese mythology and one of the three brothers born to [[Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] and his wife [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]].<ref name="Motoori2007">{{cite book |author=Norinaga Motoori |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Zk4EucRlvSwC&pg=PA191 |title=The Poetics of Motoori Norinaga: A Hermeneutical Journey |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-8248-3078-6 |page=191}}</ref><ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /><ref>{{Citation |title=Book III |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_III |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-03-09}} (called Kami-ya-wi-mimi in this source)</ref> He is best known for his role in the Assassination of [[Tagishimimi]], in which he and his brother Suizei killed Tagishimimi in order to protect Suizei from assassination.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=神八井耳命（カムヤイミミノミコト） |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2609.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}</ref>
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto was the elder brother of Suizei, and would have been expected to ascend the throne as emperor according to the lineage. However, he passed the throne on to his younger brother, Suizei, instead. This was seen as a humble and selfless act, similar to the "oath of subordination" between the brothers Yamayukihiko and Umiyukihiko.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also connected to [[Aso Shrine]], which is home to a deity named Takeban Ryujin (Taikei Watatsu). According to shrine lore, Takeban Ryujin is said to be a "child" of Kamuyaiminomikoto, and this is supported by the Kojiki, which states that "Aso-kun is a descendant of Kamuyaiminomikoto".<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> He was an ancestor of the [[Aso clan]]
In a11ition to his role in the Assassination of Tagishimimi, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also known for his courage and leadership. When the princes heard of Tagishimimi's plan to assassinate Suizei, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto immediately tried to kill Tagishimimi, but his limbs trembled and he was unable to do so. His younger brother, Kamununakahamiminomikoto, took his brother's weapon and went in to kill Tagishimimi instead.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
== Historical records == According to historical records, the Yamato Imperial Court dispatched influential members of the imperial family to the area of [[Shinano Province]] in order to control the powerful [[Suwa-no-Kami|Suwa no Kami]], who was descended from the Izumo and Koshi gods, and also because the area was a strategic point between the Sea of Japan and the East. One of these imperial family members was Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kuninomiyatsukos of Shinano and the son of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient record of Japanese history, as the one who firmly established the Emperor System.<ref name="archiveph-2023a">{{Cite web |title=archive.ph |url=https://archive.today/20230117162104/https://kodaishi.net/person/royalty/kamuyaimimi#selection-411.0-777.3 |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
The Kojiki also states that Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto appointed Takeiotatsu no Mikoto, a grandson of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, as the national governor. This appointment is believed to be the first time the king of Japan was appointed to the position of Kunizukuri. The palace of Shikinomizugaki was the palace of the 10th Emperor Sojin.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
The family of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is believed to have followed the four provincial generals, Ohiko-no-mikoto. The Shinano-no-kuninomiyatsukos are believed to have been buried in the Mori Shogunzuka Kofun, located on the opposite side of the Kawayanagi Shogunzuka Kofun.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
Hase Shrine in Shiozaki, Shinanoi, Nagano City, is dedicated to Yaimimi no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Shinano-no-kuninozukuri. After the age of Shintoism and Buddhism, the shrine became Hase Kannon. Along with Hase Temple in Yamato and Kamakura, Hase Temple is known as one of the three Hase Kannon temples in Japan. The temple was founded during the reign of Emperor Seimei (reigned 629-641), the father of Emperor Tenchi, and is said to be the oldest Hase temple in Japan.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Descendants == Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also the ancestor of a very large number of clans, as mentioned in the Kojiki. These include the Iwa-no-Kunino-no-Miyatsuko of Hitachinoku, the Naka-no-Kunino-Miyatsuko of Hitachi, the Funaki-no-Atahi of Ise, the Niwa-no-Omi of Owari, and the Shimada-no-Minamoto,<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> he is also said to be the ancestor of the [[Ō clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/743364863 |title=Nihon kodai shizoku jinmei jiten. |date=November 2010 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |others=Taro Sakamoto, Kunio Hirano, 太郎 坂本, 邦雄 平野 |isbn=978-4-642-01458-8 |chapter=神八井耳命（古代氏族） |trans-chapter=Kamuyaimimi (ancient clan) |oclc=743364863}}</ref> and the [[Aso clan]]. The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Family tree == {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}}
{{Infobox noble|reign-type=[[神武天皇]]の{{ill|Imperial Prince|ja|皇子|vi|Hoàng tử|zh|皇子|ko|황자|lt=Imperial Prince}}|birth_date=unknown|death_date=April 4th|death_place=[[Yamato Province]]|father=[[Jimmu]]|mother=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]}}
{{Nihongo|'''Kamuyaimimi'''|2=神八井耳命}}, is a figure in Japanese mythology and one of the three brothers born to [[Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] and his wife [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]].<ref name="Motoori2007">{{cite book |author=Norinaga Motoori |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Zk4EucRlvSwC&pg=PA191 |title=The Poetics of Motoori Norinaga: A Hermeneutical Journey |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-8248-3078-6 |page=191}}</ref><ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /><ref>{{Citation |title=Book III |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_III |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-03-09}} (called Kami-ya-wi-mimi in this source)</ref> He is best known for his role in the Assassination of [[Tagishimimi]], in which he and his brother Suizei killed Tagishimimi in order to protect Suizei from assassination.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=神八井耳命（カムヤイミミノミコト） |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2609.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}</ref>
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto was the elder brother of Suizei, and would have been expected to ascend the throne as emperor according to the lineage. However, he passed the throne on to his younger brother, Suizei, instead. This was seen as a humble and selfless act, similar to the "oath of subordination" between the brothers Yamayukihiko and Umiyukihiko.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also connected to [[Aso Shrine]], which is home to a deity named Takeban Ryujin (Taikei Watatsu). According to shrine lore, Takeban Ryujin is said to be a "child" of Kamuyaiminomikoto, and this is supported by the Kojiki, which states that "Aso-kun is a descendant of Kamuyaiminomikoto".<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> He was an ancestor of the [[Aso clan]]
In a11ition to his role in the Assassination of Tagishimimi, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also known for his courage and leadership. When the princes heard of Tagishimimi's plan to assassinate Suizei, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto immediately tried to kill Tagishimimi, but his limbs trembled and he was unable to do so. His younger brother, Kamununakahamiminomikoto, took his brother's weapon and went in to kill Tagishimimi instead.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
== Historical records == According to historical records, the Yamato Imperial Court dispatched influential members of the imperial family to the area of [[Shinano Province]] in order to control the powerful [[Suwa-no-Kami|Suwa no Kami]], who was descended from the Izumo and Koshi gods, and also because the area was a strategic point between the Sea of Japan and the East. One of these imperial family members was Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kuninomiyatsukos of Shinano and the son of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient record of Japanese history, as the one who firmly established the Emperor System.<ref name="archiveph-2023a">{{Cite web |title=archive.ph |url=https://archive.today/20230117162104/https://kodaishi.net/person/royalty/kamuyaimimi#selection-411.0-777.3 |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
The Kojiki also states that Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto appointed Takeiotatsu no Mikoto, a grandson of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, as the national governor. This appointment is believed to be the first time the king of Japan was appointed to the position of Kunizukuri. The palace of Shikinomizugaki was the palace of the 10th Emperor Sojin.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
The family of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is believed to have followed the four provincial generals, Ohiko-no-mikoto. The Shinano-no-kuninomiyatsukos are believed to have been buried in the Mori Shogunzuka Kofun, located on the opposite side of the Kawayanagi Shogunzuka Kofun.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
Hase Shrine in Shiozaki, Shinanoi, Nagano City, is dedicated to Yaimimi no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Shinano-no-kuninozukuri. After the age of Shintoism and Buddhism, the shrine became Hase Kannon. Along with Hase Temple in Yamato and Kamakura, Hase Temple is known as one of the three Hase Kannon temples in Japan. The temple was founded during the reign of Emperor Seimei (reigned 629-641), the father of Emperor Tenchi, and is said to be the oldest Hase temple in Japan.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Descendants == Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also the ancestor of a very large number of clans, as mentioned in the Kojiki. These include the Iwa-no-Kunino-no-Miyatsuko of Hitachinoku, the Naka-no-Kunino-Miyatsuko of Hitachi, the Funaki-no-Atahi of Ise, the Niwa-no-Omi of Owari, and the Shimada-no-Minamoto,<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> he is also said to be the ancestor of the [[Ō clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/743364863 |title=Nihon kodai shizoku jinmei jiten. |date=November 2010 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |others=Taro Sakamoto, Kunio Hirano, 太郎 坂本, 邦雄 平野 |isbn=978-4-642-01458-8 |chapter=神八井耳命（古代氏族） |trans-chapter=Kamuyaimimi (ancient clan) |oclc=743364863}}</ref> and the [[Aso clan]]. The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Family tree == {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
=== Aso genealogy === {{Tree chart/start|align=center|summary=Aso family tree}} {{Tree chart| | | | | |EmJ|EmJ=[[Emperor Jimmu]]|}} {{Tree chart| | |,|-|-|-|+|-|-|-|.| }} {{Tree chart| |MOM||DAD| |DAISY|y| Lee|MOM=Kanakori no Kami |DAD={{ill|Kamuyaimimi|lt=Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto|simple}}|DAISY=Kunitatsu no Kami |Lee=Hime Miko no Kami|}} {{Tree chart| ||!| | | |!| | | | ,| -|^|- |.|}} {{Tree chart| |Koutou | | ME |y | SIS | |SHIN|y|YAYA| |Koutou=Imperial Family Line|ME=Takeiwatatsu no Mikoto|SIS=Asotsuhime no Mikoto|SHIN=Shinhiko no Mikoto|YAYA=Yahime no Kami |}} {{Tree chart| ||||| | | |!| | | ||| ,| -|^|- |.|}} {{Tree chart| || | | | | |MOM| | | | DAD|| DAISY |MOM=Hayamikatama no Mikoto|DAD=Wakahiko no kami|DAISY=Shinhime no Mikoto}} {{Tree chart| ||||| | | |!| | | ||| !| }} {{Tree chart| |||MOM| y| DAD|| | | DAISY |MOM=Wakahime no Kami|DAD=Hikomiko no Kami|DAISY=Aso Shrine Extended Family}} {{Tree chart| ||| | ||!|}} {{Tree chart| | | | | | MOM|MOM=''Current'' Aso Shrine Priests}} {{Tree chart/end}}<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Takano |first1=Tomoaki |title=History and Festivals of the Aso Shrine |last2=Uchimura |first2=Hiroaki |publisher=Aso Shrine |year=2006 |location=Aso Shrine, Ichinomiya, Aso City.}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Hikoyai|Hikoyai-mo-mikoto]] (彦八井耳命) - Some say that he was the brother, son, or the same person as {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamuyaimimi|lt=Kamuyaimimi|ja|神八井耳命}}. Some also believe that he is the national dragon god enshrined at [[Aso Shrine]].

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}}
{{Infobox noble|reign-type=[[神武天皇]]の{{ill|Imperial Prince|ja|皇子|vi|Hoàng tử|zh|皇子|ko|황자|lt=Imperial Prince}}|birth_date=unknown|death_date=April 4th|death_place=[[Yamato Province]]|father=[[Jimmu]]|mother=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]}}
{{Nihongo|'''Kamuyaimimi'''|2=神八井耳命}}, is a figure in Japanese mythology and one of the three brothers born to [[Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] and his wife [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]].<ref name="Motoori2007">{{cite book |author=Norinaga Motoori |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Zk4EucRlvSwC&pg=PA191 |title=The Poetics of Motoori Norinaga: A Hermeneutical Journey |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-8248-3078-6 |page=191}}</ref><ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /><ref>{{Citation |title=Book III |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_III |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-03-09}} (called Kami-ya-wi-mimi in this source)</ref> He is best known for his role in the Assassination of [[Tagishimimi]], in which he and his brother Suizei killed Tagishimimi in order to protect Suizei from assassination.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=神八井耳命（カムヤイミミノミコト） |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2609.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}</ref>
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto was the elder brother of Suizei, and would have been expected to ascend the throne as emperor according to the lineage. However, he passed the throne on to his younger brother, Suizei, instead. This was seen as a humble and selfless act, similar to the "oath of subordination" between the brothers Yamayukihiko and Umiyukihiko.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also connected to [[Aso Shrine]], which is home to a deity named Takeban Ryujin (Taikei Watatsu). According to shrine lore, Takeban Ryujin is said to be a "child" of Kamuyaiminomikoto, and this is supported by the Kojiki, which states that "Aso-kun is a descendant of Kamuyaiminomikoto".<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> He was an ancestor of the [[Aso clan]]
In addition[[Category:addition]] to his role in the Assassination of Tagishimimi, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also known for his courage and leadership. When the princes heard of Tagishimimi's plan to assassinate Suizei, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto immediately tried to kill Tagishimimi, but his limbs trembled and he was unable to do so. His younger brother, Kamununakahamiminomikoto, took his brother's weapon and went in to kill Tagishimimi instead.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
== Historical records == According to historical records, the Yamato Imperial Court dispatched influential members of the imperial family to the area of [[Shinano Province]] in order to control the powerful [[Suwa-no-Kami|Suwa no Kami]], who was descended from the Izumo and Koshi gods, and also because the area was a strategic point between the Sea of Japan and the East. One of these imperial family members was Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kuninomiyatsukos of Shinano and the son of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient record of Japanese history, as the one who firmly established the Emperor System.<ref name="archiveph-2023a">{{Cite web |title=archive.ph |url=https://archive.today/20230dd7162104/https://kodaishi.net/person/royalty/kamuyaimimi#selection-4dd.0-777.3 |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
The Kojiki also states that Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto appointed Takeiotatsu no Mikoto, a grandson of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, as the national governor. This appointment is believed to be the first time the king of Japan was appointed to the position of Kunizukuri. The palace of Shikinomizugaki was the palace of the 10th Emperor Sojin.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
The family of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is believed to have followed the four provincial generals, Ohiko-no-mikoto. The Shinano-no-kuninomiyatsukos are believed to have been buried in the Mori Shogunzuka Kofun, located on the opposite side of the Kawayanagi Shogunzuka Kofun.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
Hase Shrine in Shiozaki, Shinanoi, Nagano City, is dedicated to Yaimimi no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Shinano-no-kuninozukuri. After the age of Shintoism and Buddhism, the shrine became Hase Kannon. Along with Hase Temple in Yamato and Kamakura, Hase Temple is known as one of the three Hase Kannon temples in Japan. The temple was founded during the reign of Emperor Seimei (reigned 629-641), the father of Emperor Tenchi, and is said to be the oldest Hase temple in Japan.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Descendants == Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also the ancestor of a very large number of clans, as mentioned in the Kojiki. These include the Iwa-no-Kunino-no-Miyatsuko of Hitachinoku, the Naka-no-Kunino-Miyatsuko of Hitachi, the Funaki-no-Atahi of Ise, the Niwa-no-Omi of Owari, and the Shimada-no-Minamoto,<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> he is also said to be the ancestor of the [[Ō clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/743364863 |title=Nihon kodai shizoku jinmei jiten. |date=November 2010 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |others=Taro Sakamoto, Kunio Hirano, 太郎 坂本, 邦雄 平野 |isbn=978-4-642-01458-8 |chapter=神八井耳命（古代氏族） |trans-chapter=Kamuyaimimi (ancient clan) |oclc=743364863}}</ref> and the [[Aso clan]]. The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}}
{{Infobox noble|reign-type=[[神武天皇]]の{{ill|Imperial Prince|ja|皇子|vi|Hoàng tử|zh|皇子|ko|황자|lt=Imperial Prince}}|birth_date=unknown|death_date=April 4th|death_place=[[Yamato Province]]|father=[[Jimmu]]|mother=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]}}
{{Nihongo|'''Kamuyaimimi'''|2=神八井耳命}}, is a figure in Japanese mythology and one of the three brothers born to [[Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] and his wife [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]].<ref name="Motoori2007">{{cite book |author=Norinaga Motoori |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Zk4EucRlvSwC&pg=PA191 |title=The Poetics of Motoori Norinaga: A Hermeneutical Journey |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-8248-3078-6 |page=191}}</ref><ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /><ref>{{Citation |title=Book III |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_III |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-03-09}} (called Kami-ya-wi-mimi in this source)</ref> He is best known for his role in the Assassination of [[Tagishimimi]], in which he and his brother Suizei killed Tagishimimi in order to protect Suizei from assassination.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=神八井耳命（カムヤイミミノミコト） |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2609.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}</ref>
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto was the elder brother of Suizei, and would have been expected to ascend the throne as emperor according to the lineage. However, he passed the throne on to his younger brother, Suizei, instead. This was seen as a humble and selfless act, similar to the "oath of subordination" between the brothers Yamayukihiko and Umiyukihiko.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also connected to [[Aso Shrine]], which is home to a deity named Takeban Ryujin (Taikei Watatsu). According to shrine lore, Takeban Ryujin is said to be a "child" of Kamuyaiminomikoto, and this is supported by the Kojiki, which states that "Aso-kun is a descendant of Kamuyaiminomikoto".<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> He was an ancestor of the [[Aso clan]]
In addition[[Category:addition]] to his role in the Assassination of Tagishimimi, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also known for his courage and leadership. When the princes heard of Tagishimimi's plan to assassinate Suizei, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto immediately tried to kill Tagishimimi, but his limbs trembled and he was unable to do so. His younger brother, Kamununakahamiminomikoto, took his brother's weapon and went in to kill Tagishimimi instead.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
== Historical records == According to historical records, the Yamato Imperial Court dispatched influential members of the imperial family to the area of [[Shinano Province]] in order to control the powerful [[Suwa-no-Kami|Suwa no Kami]], who was descended from the Izumo and Koshi gods, and also because the area was a strategic point between the Sea of Japan and the East. One of these imperial family members was Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kuninomiyatsukos of Shinano and the son of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient record of Japanese history, as the one who firmly established the Emperor System.<ref name="archiveph-2023a">{{Cite web |title=archive.ph |url=https://archive.today/20230dd7162104/https://kodaishi.net/person/royalty/kamuyaimimi#selection-4dd.0-777.3 |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
The Kojiki also states that Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto appointed Takeiotatsu no Mikoto, a grandson of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, as the national governor. This appointment is believed to be the first time the king of Japan was appointed to the position of Kunizukuri. The palace of Shikinomizugaki was the palace of the 10th Emperor Sojin.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
The family of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is believed to have followed the four provincial generals, Ohiko-no-mikoto. The Shinano-no-kuninomiyatsukos are believed to have been buried in the Mori Shogunzuka Kofun, located on the opposite side of the Kawayanagi Shogunzuka Kofun.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
Hase Shrine in Shiozaki, Shinanoi, Nagano City, is dedicated to Yaimimi no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Shinano-no-kuninozukuri. After the age of Shintoism and Buddhism, the shrine became Hase Kannon. Along with Hase Temple in Yamato and Kamakura, Hase Temple is known as one of the three Hase Kannon temples in Japan. The temple was founded during the reign of Emperor Seimei (reigned 629-641), the father of Emperor Tenchi, and is said to be the oldest Hase temple in Japan.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Descendants == Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also the ancestor of a very large number of clans, as mentioned in the Kojiki. These include the Iwa-no-Kunino-no-Miyatsuko of Hitachinoku, the Naka-no-Kunino-Miyatsuko of Hitachi, the Funaki-no-Atahi of Ise, the Niwa-no-Omi of Owari, and the Shimada-no-Minamoto,<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> he is also said to be the ancestor of the [[Ō clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/743364863 |title=Nihon kodai shizoku jinmei jiten. |date=November 2010 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |others=Taro Sakamoto, Kunio Hirano, 太郎 坂本, 邦雄 平野 |isbn=978-4-642-01458-8 |chapter=神八井耳命（古代氏族） |trans-chapter=Kamuyaimimi (ancient clan) |oclc=743364863}}</ref> and the [[Aso clan]]. The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}}
{{Infobox noble|reign-type=[[神武天皇]]の{{ill|Imperial Prince|ja|皇子|vi|Hoàng tử|zh|皇子|ko|황자|lt=Imperial Prince}}|birth_date=unknown|death_date=April 4th|death_place=[[Yamato Province]]|father=[[Jimmu]]|mother=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]}}
{{Nihongo|'''Kamuyaimimi'''|2=神八井耳命}}, is a figure in Japanese mythology and one of the three brothers born to [[Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] and his wife [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]].<ref name="Motoori2007">{{cite book |author=Norinaga Motoori |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Zk4EucRlvSwC&pg=PA191 |title=The Poetics of Motoori Norinaga: A Hermeneutical Journey |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-8248-3078-6 |page=191}}</ref><ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /><ref>{{Citation |title=Book III |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_III |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-03-09}} (called Kami-ya-wi-mimi in this source)</ref> He is best known for his role in the Assassination of [[Tagishimimi]], in which he and his brother Suizei killed Tagishimimi in order to protect Suizei from assassination.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=神八井耳命（カムヤイミミノミコト） |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2609.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}</ref>
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto was the elder brother of Suizei, and would have been expected to ascend the throne as emperor according to the lineage. However, he passed the throne on to his younger brother, Suizei, instead. This was seen as a humble and selfless act, similar to the "oath of subordination" between the brothers Yamayukihiko and Umiyukihiko.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also connected to [[Aso Shrine]], which is home to a deity named Takeban Ryujin (Taikei Watatsu). According to shrine lore, Takeban Ryujin is said to be a "child" of Kamuyaiminomikoto, and this is supported by the Kojiki, which states that "Aso-kun is a descendant of Kamuyaiminomikoto".<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> He was an ancestor of the [[Aso clan]]
In addition[[Category:addition]] to his role in the Assassination of Tagishimimi, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also known for his courage and leadership. When the princes heard of Tagishimimi's plan to assassinate Suizei, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto immediately tried to kill Tagishimimi, but his limbs trembled and he was unable to do so. His younger brother, Kamununakahamiminomikoto, took his brother's weapon and went in to kill Tagishimimi instead.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
== Historical records == According to historical records, the Yamato Imperial Court dispatched influential members of the imperial family to the area of [[Shinano Province]] in order to control the powerful [[Suwa-no-Kami|Suwa no Kami]], who was descended from the Izumo and Koshi gods, and also because the area was a strategic point between the Sea of Japan and the East. One of these imperial family members was Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kuninomiyatsukos of Shinano and the son of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient record of Japanese history, as the one who firmly established the Emperor System.<ref name="archiveph-2023a">{{Cite web |title=archive.ph |url=https://archive.today/20230dd7162104/https://kodaishi.net/person/royalty/kamuyaimimi#selection-4dd.0-777.3 |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
The Kojiki also states that Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto appointed Takeiotatsu no Mikoto, a grandson of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, as the national governor. This appointment is believed to be the first time the king of Japan was appointed to the position of Kunizukuri. The palace of Shikinomizugaki was the palace of the 10th Emperor Sojin.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
The family of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is believed to have followed the four provincial generals, Ohiko-no-mikoto. The Shinano-no-kuninomiyatsukos are believed to have been buried in the Mori Shogunzuka Kofun, located on the opposite side of the Kawayanagi Shogunzuka Kofun.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
Hase Shrine in Shiozaki, Shinanoi, Nagano City, is dedicated to Yaimimi no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Shinano-no-kuninozukuri. After the age of Shintoism and Buddhism, the shrine became Hase Kannon. Along with Hase Temple in Yamato and Kamakura, Hase Temple is known as one of the three Hase Kannon temples in Japan. The temple was founded during the reign of Emperor Seimei (reigned 629-641), the father of Emperor Tenchi, and is said to be the oldest Hase temple in Japan.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Descendants == Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also the ancestor of a very large number of clans, as mentioned in the Kojiki. These include the Iwa-no-Kunino-no-Miyatsuko of Hitachinoku, the Naka-no-Kunino-Miyatsuko of Hitachi, the Funaki-no-Atahi of Ise, the Niwa-no-Omi of Owari, and the Shimada-no-Minamoto,<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> he is also said to be the ancestor of the [[Ō clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/743364863 |title=Nihon kodai shizoku jinmei jiten. |date=November 2010 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |others=Taro Sakamoto, Kunio Hirano, 太郎 坂本, 邦雄 平野 |isbn=978-4-642-01458-8 |chapter=神八井耳命（古代氏族） |trans-chapter=Kamuyaimimi (ancient clan) |oclc=743364863}}</ref> and the [[Aso clan]]. The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Family tree == {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
=== Aso genealogy === {{Tree chart/start|align=center|summary=Aso family tree}} {{Tree chart| | | | | |EmJ|EmJ=[[Emperor Jimmu]]|}} {{Tree chart| | |,|-|-|-|+|-|-|-|.| }} {{Tree chart| |MOM||DAD| |DAISY|y| Lee|MOM=Kanakori no Kami |DAD={{ill|Kamuyaimimi|lt=Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto|simple}}|DAISY=Kunitatsu no Kami |Lee=Hime Miko no Kami|}} {{Tree chart| ||!| | | |!| | | | ,| -|^|- |.|}} {{Tree chart| |Koutou | | ME |y | SIS | |SHIN|y|YAYA| |Koutou=Imperial Family Line|ME=Takeiwatatsu no Mikoto|SIS=Asotsuhime no Mikoto|SHIN=Shinhiko no Mikoto|YAYA=Yahime no Kami |}} {{Tree chart| ||||| | | |!| | | ||| ,| -|^|- |.|}} {{Tree chart| || | | | | |MOM| | | | DAD|| DAISY |MOM=Hayamikatama no Mikoto|DAD=Wakahiko no kami|DAISY=Shinhime no Mikoto}} {{Tree chart| ||||| | | |!| | | ||| !| }} {{Tree chart| |||MOM| y| DAD|| | | DAISY |MOM=Wakahime no Kami|DAD=Hikomiko no Kami|DAISY=Aso Shrine Extended Family}} {{Tree chart| ||| | ||!|}} {{Tree chart| | | | | | MOM|MOM=''Current'' Aso Shrine Priests}} {{Tree chart/end}}<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Takano |first1=Tomoaki |title=History and Festivals of the Aso Shrine |last2=Uchimura |first2=Hiroaki |publisher=Aso Shrine |year=2006 |location=Aso Shrine, Ichinomiya, Aso City.}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Hikoyai|Hikoyai-mo-mikoto]] (彦八井耳命) - Some say that he was the brother, son, or the same person as {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamuyaimimi|lt=Kamuyaimimi|ja|神八井耳命}}. Some also believe that he is the national dragon god enshrined at [[Aso Shrine]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further reading ==

These include the Iwa-no-Kunino-no-Miyatsuko of Hitachinoku, the Naka-no-Kunino-Miyatsuko of Hitachi, the Funaki-no-Atahi of Ise, the Niwa-no-Omi of Owari, and the Shimada-no-Minamoto,<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> he is also said to be the ancestor of the [[Ō clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/743364863 |title=Nihon kodai shizoku jinmei jiten. |date=November 2010 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |others=Taro Sakamoto, Kunio Hirano, 太郎 坂本, 邦雄 平野 |isbn=978-4-642-01458-8 |chapter=神八井耳命（古代氏族） |trans-chapter=Kamuyaimimi (ancient clan) |oclc=743364863}}</ref> and the [[Aso clan]]. The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

[[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:People of Jōmon-period Japan]] [[Category:Aso clan]] [[Category:Ō clan]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko position holders]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: 5]][[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Kamuyaimimi]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] {{Aso Faith}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Kamuyaimimi]][[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}}
{{Infobox noble|reign-type=[[神武天皇]]の{{ill|Imperial Prince|ja|皇子|vi|Hoàng tử|zh|皇子|ko|황자|lt=Imperial Prince}}|birth_date=unknown|death_date=April 4th|death_place=[[Yamato Province]]|father=[[Jimmu]]|mother=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]}}
{{Nihongo|'''Kamuyaimimi'''|2=神八井耳命}}, is a figure in Japanese mythology and one of the three brothers born to [[Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] and his wife [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]].<ref name="Motoori2007">{{cite book |author=Norinaga Motoori |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Zk4EucRlvSwC&pg=PA191 |title=The Poetics of Motoori Norinaga: A Hermeneutical Journey |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-8248-3078-6 |page=191}}</ref><ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /><ref>{{Citation |title=Book III |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_III |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-03-09}} (called Kami-ya-wi-mimi in this source)</ref> He is best known for his role in the Assassination of [[Tagishimimi]], in which he and his brother Suizei killed Tagishimimi in order to protect Suizei from assassination.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=神八井耳命（カムヤイミミノミコト） |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2609.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}</ref>
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto was the elder brother of Suizei, and would have been expected to ascend the throne as emperor according to the lineage. However, he passed the throne on to his younger brother, Suizei, instead. This was seen as a humble and selfless act, similar to the "oath of subordination" between the brothers Yamayukihiko and Umiyukihiko.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also connected to [[Aso Shrine]], which is home to a deity named Takeban Ryujin (Taikei Watatsu). According to shrine lore, Takeban Ryujin is said to be a "child" of Kamuyaiminomikoto, and this is supported by the Kojiki, which states that "Aso-kun is a descendant of Kamuyaiminomikoto".<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> He was an ancestor of the [[Aso clan]]
In addition[[Category:addition]] to his role in the Assassination of Tagishimimi, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also known for his courage and leadership. When the princes heard of Tagishimimi's plan to assassinate Suizei, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto immediately tried to kill Tagishimimi, but his limbs trembled and he was unable to do so. His younger brother, Kamununakahamiminomikoto, took his brother's weapon and went in to kill Tagishimimi instead.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />
== Historical records == According to historical records, the Yamato Imperial Court dispatched influential members of the imperial family to the area of [[Shinano Province]] in order to control the powerful [[Suwa-no-Kami|Suwa no Kami]], who was descended from the Izumo and Koshi gods, and also because the area was a strategic point between the Sea of Japan and the East. One of these imperial family members was Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kuninomiyatsukos of Shinano and the son of [[Emperor Jimmu]]. Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient record of Japanese history, as the one who firmly established the Emperor System.<ref name="archiveph-2023a">{{Cite web |title=archive.ph |url=https://archive.today/20230dd7162104/https://kodaishi.net/person/royalty/kamuyaimimi#selection-4dd.0-777.3 |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
The Kojiki also states that Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto appointed Takeiotatsu no Mikoto, a grandson of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, as the national governor. This appointment is believed to be the first time the king of Japan was appointed to the position of Kunizukuri. The palace of Shikinomizugaki was the palace of the 10th Emperor Sojin.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
The family of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is believed to have followed the four provincial generals, Ohiko-no-mikoto. The Shinano-no-kuninomiyatsukos are believed to have been buried in the Mori Shogunzuka Kofun, located on the opposite side of the Kawayanagi Shogunzuka Kofun.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
Hase Shrine in Shiozaki, Shinanoi, Nagano City, is dedicated to Yaimimi no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Shinano-no-kuninozukuri. After the age of Shintoism and Buddhism, the shrine became Hase Kannon. Along with Hase Temple in Yamato and Kamakura, Hase Temple is known as one of the three Hase Kannon temples in Japan. The temple was founded during the reign of Emperor Seimei (reigned 629-641), the father of Emperor Tenchi, and is said to be the oldest Hase temple in Japan.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Descendants == Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also the ancestor of a very large number of clans, as mentioned in the Kojiki. These include the Iwa-no-Kunino-no-Miyatsuko of Hitachinoku, the Naka-no-Kunino-Miyatsuko of Hitachi, the Funaki-no-Atahi of Ise, the Niwa-no-Omi of Owari, and the Shimada-no-Minamoto,<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> he is also said to be the ancestor of the [[Ō clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/743364863 |title=Nihon kodai shizoku jinmei jiten. |date=November 2010 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |others=Taro Sakamoto, Kunio Hirano, 太郎 坂本, 邦雄 平野 |isbn=978-4-642-01458-8 |chapter=神八井耳命（古代氏族） |trans-chapter=Kamuyaimimi (ancient clan) |oclc=743364863}}</ref> and the [[Aso clan]]. The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
== Family tree == {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
=== Aso genealogy === {{Tree chart/start|align=center|summary=Aso family tree}} {{Tree chart| | | | | |EmJ|EmJ=[[Emperor Jimmu]]|}} {{Tree chart| | |,|-|-|-|+|-|-|-|.| }} {{Tree chart| |MOM||DAD| |DAISY|y| Lee|MOM=Kanakori no Kami |DAD={{ill|Kamuyaimimi|lt=Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto|simple}}|DAISY=Kunitatsu no Kami |Lee=Hime Miko no Kami|}} {{Tree chart| ||!| | | |!| | | | ,| -|^|- |.|}} {{Tree chart| |Koutou | | ME |y | SIS | |SHIN|y|YAYA| |Koutou=Imperial Family Line|ME=Takeiwatatsu no Mikoto|SIS=Asotsuhime no Mikoto|SHIN=Shinhiko no Mikoto|YAYA=Yahime no Kami |}} {{Tree chart| ||||| | | |!| | | ||| ,| -|^|- |.|}} {{Tree chart| || | | | | |MOM| | | | DAD|| DAISY |MOM=Hayamikatama no Mikoto|DAD=Wakahiko no kami|DAISY=Shinhime no Mikoto}} {{Tree chart| ||||| | | |!| | | ||| !| }} {{Tree chart| |||MOM| y| DAD|| | | DAISY |MOM=Wakahime no Kami|DAD=Hikomiko no Kami|DAISY=Aso Shrine Extended Family}} {{Tree chart| ||| | ||!|}} {{Tree chart| | | | | | MOM|MOM=''Current'' Aso Shrine Priests}} {{Tree chart/end}}<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Takano |first1=Tomoaki |title=History and Festivals of the Aso Shrine |last2=Uchimura |first2=Hiroaki |publisher=Aso Shrine |year=2006 |location=Aso Shrine, Ichinomiya, Aso City.}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Hikoyai|Hikoyai-mo-mikoto]] (彦八井耳命) - Some say that he was the brother, son, or the same person as {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamuyaimimi|lt=Kamuyaimimi|ja|神八井耳命}}. Some also believe that he is the national dragon god enshrined at [[Aso Shrine]].

{{Nihongo|'''Kamuyaimimi'''|2=神八井耳命}}, is a figure in Japanese mythology and one of the three brothers born to [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] and his wife [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]].<ref name="Motoori2007">{{cite book |author=Norinaga Motoori |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Zk4EucRlvSwC&pg=PA191 |title=The Poetics of Motoori Norinaga: A Hermeneutical Journey |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-8248-3078-6 |page=191}}</ref><ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /><ref>{{Citation |title=Book III |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_III |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-03-09}} (called Kami-ya-wi-mimi in this source)</ref> He is best known for his role in the Assassination of [[:en:Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi]], in which he and his brother Suizei killed Tagishimimi in order to protect Suizei from assassination.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=神八井耳命（カムヤイミミノミコト） |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2609.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}</ref>

According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto was the elder brother of Suizei, and would have been expected to ascend the throne as emperor according to the lineage. However, he passed the throne on to his younger brother, Suizei, instead. This was seen as a humble and selfless act, similar to the "oath of subordination" between the brothers Yamayukihiko and Umiyukihiko.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />

Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also connected to [[Aso Shrine]], which is home to a deity named Takeban Ryujin (Taikei Watatsu). According to shrine lore, Takeban Ryujin is said to be a "child" of Kamuyaiminomikoto, and this is supported by the Kojiki, which states that "Aso-kun is a descendant of Kamuyaiminomikoto".<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> He was an ancestor of the [[Aso clan]]

In addition to his role in the Assassination of Tagishimimi, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also known for his courage and leadership. When the princes heard of Tagishimimi's plan to assassinate Suizei, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto immediately tried to kill Tagishimimi, but his limbs trembled and he was unable to do so. His younger brother, Kamununakahamiminomikoto, took his brother's weapon and went in to kill Tagishimimi instead.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />

== Historical records ==
According to historical records, the Yamato Imperial Court dispatched influential members of the imperial family to the area of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] in order to control the powerful [[:en:Suwa-no-Kami|Suwa no Kami]], who was descended from the Izumo and Koshi gods, and also because the area was a strategic point between the Sea of Japan and the East. One of these imperial family members was Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kuninomiyatsukos of Shinano and the son of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient record of Japanese history, as the one who firmly established the Emperor System.<ref name="archiveph-2023a">{{Cite web |title=archive.ph |url=https://archive.today/20230dd7162104/https://kodaishi.net/person/royalty/kamuyaimimi#selection-4dd.0-777.3 |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>

The Kojiki also states that Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto appointed Takeiotatsu no Mikoto, a grandson of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, as the national governor. This appointment is believed to be the first time the king of Japan was appointed to the position of Kunizukuri. The palace of Shikinomizugaki was the palace of the 10th Emperor Sojin.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

The family of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is believed to have followed the four provincial generals, Ohiko-no-mikoto. The Shinano-no-kuninomiyatsukos are believed to have been buried in the Mori Shogunzuka Kofun, located on the opposite side of the Kawayanagi Shogunzuka Kofun.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

Hase Shrine in Shiozaki, Shinanoi, Nagano City, is dedicated to Yaimimi no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Shinano-no-kuninozukuri. After the age of Shintoism and Buddhism, the shrine became Hase Kannon. Along with Hase Temple in Yamato and Kamakura, Hase Temple is known as one of the three Hase Kannon temples in Japan. The temple was founded during the reign of Emperor Seimei (reigned 629-641), the father of Emperor Tenchi, and is said to be the oldest Hase temple in Japan.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

== Descendants ==
Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also the ancestor of a very large number of clans, as mentioned in the Kojiki. These include the Iwa-no-Kunino-no-Miyatsuko of Hitachinoku, the Naka-no-Kunino-Miyatsuko of Hitachi, the Funaki-no-Atahi of Ise, the Niwa-no-Omi of Owari, and the Shimada-no-Minamoto,<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> he is also said to be the ancestor of the [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/743364863 |title=Nihon kodai shizoku jinmei jiten. |date=November 2010 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |others=Taro Sakamoto, Kunio Hirano, 太郎 坂本, 邦雄 平野 |isbn=978-4-642-01458-8 |chapter=神八井耳命（古代氏族） |trans-chapter=Kamuyaimimi (ancient clan) |oclc=743364863}}</ref> and the [[Aso clan]]. The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

{{wikidata link|Q11588756}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kamuyaimimi]][[fr:Kamuyaimimi]][[ja:神八井耳命]][[ko:카무야이미미노미코토]][[simple:Kamuyaimimi]][[zh:神八井耳命]]
{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Takeiwatatsu no Mikoto|WD=Q10888904}}|father={{ill|Jimmu|WD=Q200188}}|mother={{ill|Himetataraisuzuhime|WD=Q5353793}}|relatives={{ill|Emperor Suizei|WD=Q313321}}; {{ill|Hikoyai-no-mikoto|WD=Q11086120}}; {{ill|Kishimi Mino Mikoto|WD=Q13257020}}; {{ill|Tagishimimi|WD=Q11075750}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11588756]]

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}}

{{Infobox noble|reign-type=[[神武天皇]]の{{ill|Imperial Prince|ja|皇子|vi|Hoàng tử|zh|皇子|ko|황자|lt=Imperial Prince|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Prince|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|birth_date=unknown|death_date=April 4th|death_place=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|father=[[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]|mother=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]}}

{{Nihongo|'''Kamuyaimimi'''|2=神八井耳命}}, is a figure in Japanese mythology and one of the three brothers born to [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] and his wife [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]].<ref name="Motoori2007">{{cite book |author=Norinaga Motoori |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Zk4EucRlvSwC&pg=PA191 |title=The Poetics of Motoori Norinaga: A Hermeneutical Journey |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-8248-3078-6 |page=191}}</ref><ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /><ref>{{Citation |title=Book III |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_III |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-03-09}} (called Kami-ya-wi-mimi in this source)</ref> He is best known for his role in the Assassination of [[:en:Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi]], in which he and his brother Suizei killed Tagishimimi in order to protect Suizei from assassination.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=神八井耳命（カムヤイミミノミコト） |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2609.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}</ref>

According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto was the elder brother of Suizei, and would have been expected to ascend the throne as emperor according to the lineage. However, he passed the throne on to his younger brother, Suizei, instead. This was seen as a humble and selfless act, similar to the "oath of subordination" between the brothers Yamayukihiko and Umiyukihiko.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />

Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also connected to [[Aso Shrine]], which is home to a deity named Takeban Ryujin (Taikei Watatsu). According to shrine lore, Takeban Ryujin is said to be a "child" of Kamuyaiminomikoto, and this is supported by the Kojiki, which states that "Aso-kun is a descendant of Kamuyaiminomikoto".<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> He was an ancestor of the [[Aso clan]]

In addition to his role in the Assassination of Tagishimimi, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also known for his courage and leadership. When the princes heard of Tagishimimi's plan to assassinate Suizei, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto immediately tried to kill Tagishimimi, but his limbs trembled and he was unable to do so. His younger brother, Kamununakahamiminomikoto, took his brother's weapon and went in to kill Tagishimimi instead.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />

== Historical records ==
According to historical records, the Yamato Imperial Court dispatched influential members of the imperial family to the area of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] in order to control the powerful [[:en:Suwa-no-Kami|Suwa no Kami]], who was descended from the Izumo and Koshi gods, and also because the area was a strategic point between the Sea of Japan and the East. One of these imperial family members was Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kuninomiyatsukos of Shinano and the son of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient record of Japanese history, as the one who firmly established the Emperor System.<ref name="archiveph-2023a">{{Cite web |title=archive.ph |url=https://archive.today/20230dd7162104/https://kodaishi.net/person/royalty/kamuyaimimi#selection-4dd.0-777.3 |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>

The Kojiki also states that Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto appointed Takeiotatsu no Mikoto, a grandson of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, as the national governor. This appointment is believed to be the first time the king of Japan was appointed to the position of Kunizukuri. The palace of Shikinomizugaki was the palace of the 10th Emperor Sojin.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

The family of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is believed to have followed the four provincial generals, Ohiko-no-mikoto. The Shinano-no-kuninomiyatsukos are believed to have been buried in the Mori Shogunzuka Kofun, located on the opposite side of the Kawayanagi Shogunzuka Kofun.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

Hase Shrine in Shiozaki, Shinanoi, Nagano City, is dedicated to Yaimimi no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Shinano-no-kuninozukuri. After the age of Shintoism and Buddhism, the shrine became Hase Kannon. Along with Hase Temple in Yamato and Kamakura, Hase Temple is known as one of the three Hase Kannon temples in Japan. The temple was founded during the reign of Emperor Seimei (reigned 629-641), the father of Emperor Tenchi, and is said to be the oldest Hase temple in Japan.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

== Descendants ==
Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also the ancestor of a very large number of clans, as mentioned in the Kojiki. These include the Iwa-no-Kunino-no-Miyatsuko of Hitachinoku, the Naka-no-Kunino-Miyatsuko of Hitachi, the Funaki-no-Atahi of Ise, the Niwa-no-Omi of Owari, and the Shimada-no-Minamoto,<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> he is also said to be the ancestor of the [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/743364863 |title=Nihon kodai shizoku jinmei jiten. |date=November 2010 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |others=Taro Sakamoto, Kunio Hirano, 太郎 坂本, 邦雄 平野 |isbn=978-4-642-01458-8 |chapter=神八井耳命（古代氏族） |trans-chapter=Kamuyaimimi (ancient clan) |oclc=743364863}}</ref> and the [[Aso clan]]. The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}}

{{Infobox noble|reign-type=[[神武天皇]]の{{ill|Imperial Prince|ja|皇子|vi|Hoàng tử|zh|皇子|ko|황자|lt=Imperial Prince|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Prince|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|birth_date=unknown|death_date=April 4th|death_place=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|father=[[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]|mother=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]}}

{{Nihongo|'''Kamuyaimimi'''|2=神八井耳命}}, is a figure in Japanese mythology and one of the three brothers born to [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] and his wife [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]].<ref name="Motoori2007">{{cite book |author=Norinaga Motoori |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Zk4EucRlvSwC&pg=PA191 |title=The Poetics of Motoori Norinaga: A Hermeneutical Journey |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-8248-3078-6 |page=191}}</ref><ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /><ref>{{Citation |title=Book III |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_III |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-03-09}} (called Kami-ya-wi-mimi in this source)</ref> He is best known for his role in the Assassination of [[:en:Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi]], in which he and his brother Suizei killed Tagishimimi in order to protect Suizei from assassination.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=神八井耳命（カムヤイミミノミコト） |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2609.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}</ref>

According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto was the elder brother of Suizei, and would have been expected to ascend the throne as emperor according to the lineage. However, he passed the throne on to his younger brother, Suizei, instead. This was seen as a humble and selfless act, similar to the "oath of subordination" between the brothers Yamayukihiko and Umiyukihiko.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />

Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also connected to [[Aso Shrine]], which is home to a deity named Takeban Ryujin (Taikei Watatsu). According to shrine lore, Takeban Ryujin is said to be a "child" of Kamuyaiminomikoto, and this is supported by the Kojiki, which states that "Aso-kun is a descendant of Kamuyaiminomikoto".<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> He was an ancestor of the [[Aso clan]]

In addition to his role in the Assassination of Tagishimimi, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also known for his courage and leadership. When the princes heard of Tagishimimi's plan to assassinate Suizei, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto immediately tried to kill Tagishimimi, but his limbs trembled and he was unable to do so. His younger brother, Kamununakahamiminomikoto, took his brother's weapon and went in to kill Tagishimimi instead.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />

== Historical records ==
According to historical records, the Yamato Imperial Court dispatched influential members of the imperial family to the area of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] in order to control the powerful [[:en:Suwa-no-Kami|Suwa no Kami]], who was descended from the Izumo and Koshi gods, and also because the area was a strategic point between the Sea of Japan and the East. One of these imperial family members was Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kuninomiyatsukos of Shinano and the son of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient record of Japanese history, as the one who firmly established the Emperor System.<ref name="archiveph-2023a">{{Cite web |title=archive.ph |url=https://archive.today/20230dd7162104/https://kodaishi.net/person/royalty/kamuyaimimi#selection-4dd.0-777.3 |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>

The Kojiki also states that Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto appointed Takeiotatsu no Mikoto, a grandson of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, as the national governor. This appointment is believed to be the first time the king of Japan was appointed to the position of Kunizukuri. The palace of Shikinomizugaki was the palace of the 10th Emperor Sojin.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

The family of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is believed to have followed the four provincial generals, Ohiko-no-mikoto. The Shinano-no-kuninomiyatsukos are believed to have been buried in the Mori Shogunzuka Kofun, located on the opposite side of the Kawayanagi Shogunzuka Kofun.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

Hase Shrine in Shiozaki, Shinanoi, Nagano City, is dedicated to Yaimimi no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Shinano-no-kuninozukuri. After the age of Shintoism and Buddhism, the shrine became Hase Kannon. Along with Hase Temple in Yamato and Kamakura, Hase Temple is known as one of the three Hase Kannon temples in Japan. The temple was founded during the reign of Emperor Seimei (reigned 629-641), the father of Emperor Tenchi, and is said to be the oldest Hase temple in Japan.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

== Descendants ==
Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also the ancestor of a very large number of clans, as mentioned in the Kojiki. These include the Iwa-no-Kunino-no-Miyatsuko of Hitachinoku, the Naka-no-Kunino-Miyatsuko of Hitachi, the Funaki-no-Atahi of Ise, the Niwa-no-Omi of Owari, and the Shimada-no-Minamoto,<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> he is also said to be the ancestor of the [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/743364863 |title=Nihon kodai shizoku jinmei jiten. |date=November 2010 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |others=Taro Sakamoto, Kunio Hirano, 太郎 坂本, 邦雄 平野 |isbn=978-4-642-01458-8 |chapter=神八井耳命（古代氏族） |trans-chapter=Kamuyaimimi (ancient clan) |oclc=743364863}}</ref> and the [[Aso clan]]. The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

However, he passed the throne on to his younger brother, Suizei, instead. This was seen as a humble and selfless act, similar to the "oath of subordination" between the brothers Yamayukihiko and Umiyukihiko.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />

Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also connected to [[Aso Shrine]], which is home to a deity named Takeban Ryujin (Taikei Watatsu). According to shrine lore, Takeban Ryujin is said to be a "child" of Kamuyaiminomikoto, and this is supported by the Kojiki, which states that "Aso-kun is a descendant of Kamuyaiminomikoto".<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> He was an ancestor of the [[Aso clan]]

In addition to his role in the Assassination of Tagishimimi, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also known for his courage and leadership. When the princes heard of Tagishimimi's plan to assassinate Suizei, Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto immediately tried to kill Tagishimimi, but his limbs trembled and he was unable to do so. His younger brother, Kamununakahamiminomikoto, took his brother's weapon and went in to kill Tagishimimi instead.<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" />

== Historical records ==
According to historical records, the Yamato Imperial Court dispatched influential members of the imperial family to the area of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] in order to control the powerful [[:en:Suwa-no-Kami|Suwa no Kami]], who was descended from the Izumo and Koshi gods, and also because the area was a strategic point between the Sea of Japan and the East. One of these imperial family members was Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kuninomiyatsukos of Shinano and the son of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]. Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient record of Japanese history, as the one who firmly established the Emperor System.<ref name="archiveph-2023a">{{Cite web |title=archive.ph |url=https://archive.today/20230dd7162104/https://kodaishi.net/person/royalty/kamuyaimimi#selection-4dd.0-777.3 |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>

The Kojiki also states that Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto appointed Takeiotatsu no Mikoto, a grandson of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto, as the national governor. This appointment is believed to be the first time the king of Japan was appointed to the position of Kunizukuri. The palace of Shikinomizugaki was the palace of the 10th Emperor Sojin.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

The family of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is believed to have followed the four provincial generals, Ohiko-no-mikoto. The Shinano-no-kuninomiyatsukos are believed to have been buried in the Mori Shogunzuka Kofun, located on the opposite side of the Kawayanagi Shogunzuka Kofun.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

Hase Shrine in Shiozaki, Shinanoi, Nagano City, is dedicated to Yaimimi no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Shinano-no-kuninozukuri. After the age of Shintoism and Buddhism, the shrine became Hase Kannon. Along with Hase Temple in Yamato and Kamakura, Hase Temple is known as one of the three Hase Kannon temples in Japan. The temple was founded during the reign of Emperor Seimei (reigned 629-641), the father of Emperor Tenchi, and is said to be the oldest Hase temple in Japan.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

== Descendants ==
Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto is also the ancestor of a very large number of clans, as mentioned in the Kojiki. These include the Iwa-no-Kunino-no-Miyatsuko of Hitachinoku, the Naka-no-Kunino-Miyatsuko of Hitachi, the Funaki-no-Atahi of Ise, the Niwa-no-Omi of Owari, and the Shimada-no-Minamoto,<ref name="nihonsinwacom-2023a" /> he is also said to be the ancestor of the [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/743364863 |title=Nihon kodai shizoku jinmei jiten. |date=November 2010 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |others=Taro Sakamoto, Kunio Hirano, 太郎 坂本, 邦雄 平野 |isbn=978-4-642-01458-8 |chapter=神八井耳命（古代氏族） |trans-chapter=Kamuyaimimi (ancient clan) |oclc=743364863}}</ref> and the [[Aso clan]]. The descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto have left traces all over Japan, from Kyushu to the [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tohoku]] region. They supported and solidified the "Emperor System" founded by Emperor Jinmu. Taian Manpo, who compiled the Kojiki, is a descendant of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />

According to the Kojiki, Kamuyaimi-no-mikoto passed the imperial throne to his younger brother. The descendants of Kamuyaimi-no-Mikoto were in charge of the rituals at [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]] Shrine, which are considered the oldest power spots in the east and west of the Japanese archipelago.<ref name="archiveph-2023a" />
